coredata.dc:title: One-pot alkali cutting-assisted synthesis of fluorescence tunable amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots as a multifunctional nanosensor for sensing of pH and tannic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xueying
Yu, Jiayu
Yan, Yongcun
Song, Weiling
Hai, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a one-pot alkali cutting-assisted synthesis approach has been developed to gain fluorescence (FL) tunable amino functionalized GQDs (NH2-GQDs), which exhibit concentration- and excitation-dependent FL behaviors, due to the self-assembled J-type aggregation effect and different electronic transitions governed by graphene basal plane and functional groups. While NH2-GQDs possess brighter FL emission than pristine GQDs, owning to the functionalization of amino groups with strong electron withdrawing ability. Particularly, the pH-dependent FL behavior of NH2-GQDs further reflects the FL emission mechanism originated from the intrinsic zigzag sites and introduced amino and carboxylic groups, which is available for pH sensing. Moreover, the NH2-GQDs also show a FL quenching upon reaction with tannic acid (TA), resulting in the construction of a FL turn-off TA sensing platform. A good linear relationship is obtained between logarithm of FL intensity (log F) and TA concentration in a linear dynamic range of 1–40 μM and a limit of detection of 43.3 nM (3σ/s, n = 9) is achieved, with a precision of 0.08% RSD at a concentration level of 5 μM (n = 9). This work features a simple and direct approach to acquire multifunctional nanosensor, providing great potential for further applications in chem/biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Alkali cutting
Tunable fluorescence
Multifunctional nanosensor
pH detection
Tannic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of NH2-GQDs toward pH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully appreciate the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22004077 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2020M671988 ), the Open Project of Chemistry Department of Qingdao University of Science and Technology ( QUSTHX201928 ), and the Open Project of Shandong Key Laboratory of Tumor Marker Detection Technology ( KLDTTM2019-4 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) GQDs and (B) NH2-GQDs. Inset: size distribution. AFM images of (C) GQDs and (D) NH2-GQDs. Inset: height profile along the line from a to b. (E) FT-IR spectra of GQDs and NH2-GQDs. High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of (F) GQDs, (G) NH2-GQDs. (H) N1s XPS spectra of NH2-GQDs.

AFM images of NH2-GQDs solutions with different concentrations (A) 0.25, (B) 1.0, (C) 5.0 mg mL−1. Inset: height profile along the line from a to b. (D) Schematics for the aggregation of NH2-GQDs with the increase of the concentration.

FL spectra (A); the linear FL response (B) of NH2-GQDs; FL spectra (C); the FL response (D) of GQDs under different pH conditions (pH 3.0–10.0). The excitation wavelength is 280 nm under a PMT voltage of 500 V and slit of 10 nm, and the emission wavelength at 430 nm is recorded.

(A) FL spectra of NH2-GQDs treated with various concentration of TA (0–50 μM); (B) Plot of F versus TA concentration at optimal conditions; Inset: linear calibration graphs between log F and TA concentration within 1–40 μM. The excitation wavelength is 280 nm under a PMT voltage of 500 V and slit of 10 nm, and the emission wavelength at 430 nm is recorded.
Schematics for preparation of NH2-GQDs and their applications in sensing of pH and TA.

head.ce:title: One-pot alkali cutting-assisted synthesis of fluorescence tunable amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots as a multifunctional nanosensor for sensing of pH and tannic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Intelligent and Wearable Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Intelligent and Wearable Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Intelligent and Wearable Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science in Universities of Shandong, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis
Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science in Universities of Shandong
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science in Universities of Shandong, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266042, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a one-pot alkali cutting-assisted synthesis approach has been developed to gain fluorescence (FL) tunable amino functionalized GQDs (NH2-GQDs), which exhibit concentration- and excitation-dependent FL behaviors, due to the self-assembled J-type aggregation effect and different electronic transitions governed by graphene basal plane and functional groups. While NH2-GQDs possess brighter FL emission than pristine GQDs, owning to the functionalization of amino groups with strong electron withdrawing ability. Particularly, the pH-dependent FL behavior of NH2-GQDs further reflects the FL emission mechanism originated from the intrinsic zigzag sites and introduced amino and carboxylic groups, which is available for pH sensing. Moreover, the NH2-GQDs also show a FL quenching upon reaction with tannic acid (TA), resulting in the construction of a FL turn-off TA sensing platform. A good linear relationship is obtained between logarithm of FL intensity (log F) and TA concentration in a linear dynamic range of 1–40 μM and a limit of detection of 43.3 nM (3σ/s, n = 9) is achieved, with a precision of 0.08% RSD at a concentration level of 5 μM (n = 9). This work features a simple and direct approach to acquire multifunctional nanosensor, providing great potential for further applications in chem/biosensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a class of graphene nanosheets of less than 100 nm in lateral size. Due to the quantum confinement effect and edge effect, GQDs possess more special physicochemical properties including tunable bandgaps and surface functionalities, high chemical and photostabilities, favorable biocompatibility and unique optical properties (such as UV–vis absorption and fluorescence) [1]. These unique characteristics render GQDs promising candidate for extensive application prospects covering chem/biosensing, bioimaging, biomedicine and even energy conversion [2,3]. It is noteworthy that the FL of GQDs has attracted great attention in sensing fields, of which mechanism may be attributed to electron-hole recombination, quantum-size effect/free zigzag sites with a carbene-like triplet ground state [4]. However, most of the preparation procedures are time-consuming with low production yield, and the obtained pristine GQDs usually suffer from insufficient FL emission. Thus, the development of effective methods to obtain GQDs with tunable improved FL intensity is an urgent issue. Surface functionalization provides an alternative approach to modulate the emission color and efficiency of GQDs [5]. For instance, as an electron donor, amino groups can be covalently bonded to GQDs, facilitating the transformation from the ammonium moiety to the conjugated structure, which will improve the FL performance of GQDs with enhanced intensity [5–7], making GQDs a more appropriate probe with superior sensitivity, selectivity, rapidity, and applicability.

It is well known that pH value plays a pivotal role in wide fields including chemical, biological, industrial and environmental systems [8]. Notably, a slight change in pH value will cause extensively influence on the stability and safety of environment including drinking water and food [9]. Besides, the biological systems can also be affected by the minor variation of pH, causing physiological dysfunction, wound infection, and even tumor generation [10,11]. Therefore, the accurate detection of the pH is essential to investigate the environmental quality, physiological and pathological processes, etc. Previous studies have proved that the FL intensities of GQDs can also be influenced significantly when the pH changes owning to the protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups [12,13]. This indicates that the FL probes of GQDs also exhibit a pH-sensitive performance and bear great potential in pH sensing.

As a typical and natural polyphenolic compound with a central glucose and 10 galloyl groups [14], tannic acid (TA) possesses unique chemical and physiological characteristics such as antimutagenic, anticarcinogenic, antioxidant and radical scavenging activities, and can combine with metal ions, polysaccharide or proteins via either complexation or electrostatic interaction [15]. It is reported that TA participates in the antibacterial and antineoplastic process, of which content will be varied significantly in these physiological activities. Thus, TA can serve as an important component in medicinal products. However, higher level of TA in human bodies will cause hepatic problems such as liver function failure. Moreover, TA also serves as food additive in alcoholic beverages and astringents for cosmetics purposes [16]. As one of the main persistent organic pollutants in leather industrial sewage, TA can induce serious harm to the environment [17]. Thus, it is of vital importance to accurate monitoring TA content in biosome, foods and even environment. A variety of methods for the determination of TA have been proposed including HPLC [18], electrochemical [19], chemiluminescence [20] and spectrophotometry [21], while tedious procedure, poor sensitivity and selectivity hinder their further application in real samples. Compared with these techniques, fluorometry exhibit the merits of the simple operation, high sensitivity and selectivity, thus FL probes have been designed and attracted great attention for the determination of TA.

Although a lot of effort has been devoted to synthesize GQDs for the FL detection, the efficient one-step synthesis for the rapid production of GQDs is still a challenge. Considering the vital role of amino groups in surface modification and regulation of the optical properties of GQDs, herein we report a straightforward synthesis of amino functionalized GQDs (NH2-GQDs) via a one-step alkali-assisted hydrothermal cutting approach, using graphene oxide (GO) as precursor and amino groups as a modifier (Scheme 1 ). The alkali cutting combined with the oxidant hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) promotes the formation of abundant zigzag sites on the edge of GQDs, which serves as the FL emission origin. Meanwhile, the amino groups are covalently modified on the surface of GQDs through amidation between ammonia and carboxyl groups. Remarkably, the NH2-GQDs exhibit intriguing concentration-dependent FL behavior, in which the concentration increase will induce the redshift emission with improved intensity, due to the self-assembled J-type aggregation effect [22]. Besides, an excitation-dependent FL performance is also observed in NH2-GQDs, which is resulted from the different types of electronic transitions governed by graphene basal plane and different functional groups. What's more, the as-prepared NH2-GQDs exhibit higher FL emission than pristine GQDs, due to the introduction of amino groups with strong electron withdrawing ability. Particularly, the specific pH-dependent FL behavior of NH2-GQDs can reflect that the FL emission mechanism is derived from the intrinsic zigzag sites and introduced functional amino and carboxylic groups, which will be protonated and deprotonated upon changing pH values. Hence, the NH2-GQDs are available for pH sensing. Moreover, the high FL intensity of NH2-GQDs can be rapidly quenched upon the addition of TA, which might be ascribed to the formation of NH2-GQDs-TA complex via hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking and electrostatic interaction [23,24]. Therefore, a FL turn-off sensing platform is proposed for TA determination. This proposed FL probe shows obvious selectivity towards TA, and has been successfully applied to real sample detection. Overall, a multifunctional FL nanosensor of NH2-GQDs is exploited based on the simple and efficient one-pot alkali cutting approach, which provides opportunity for construction of versatile biosensing platform.

Graphite powder, ammonia (NH3·H2O), potassium hydroxide (KOH), tannic acid (TA) and other common reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Deionized water of 18 MΩ cm was used throughout.

GO was firstly synthesized according to the modified Hummers and Offeman's method [25]. Then the GQDs and NH2-GQDs were prepared based on our previous report with some modification [26]. Typically, the GO powder was dispersed in KOH solution (1 M) under the ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Afterwards 0.5 mL of NH3·H2O was dropped into 2 mL dispersion of GO. Subsequently, 0.5 mL 30% H2O2 and 2.5 mL water were injected into the mixture dropwise. The solution was stirred for 15 min and transferred into a 25 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h in an electric oven. After cooling to room temperature, the products were filtered through a 0.1 μm microporous membrane to remove the precipitate, and then unreacted NH3·H2O and H2O2 were removed via rotary evaporation. GQDs were prepared using the same procedure above without the using of NH3·H2O.

The obtained solution was adjusted to pH 7.0 and dialyzed over deionized water in a dialysis bag (MWCO membranes 100–500 Da) for 1 days to remove the residual reactants. Finally, the powder of NH2-GQDs was quantified by lyophilization and re-dispersed in pure water for further use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100 F field-emission electron microscope operating at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). The size distribution histogram is obtained by measuring and counting the particle size in TEM images using Nano Measurer 1.2 software. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were acquired on a Bruker Dimension icon atomic force microscopy under ScanAsyst in air mode with a tip type of ScanAsyst-air (Bruker, Germany). The height profile along the line from a to b in AFM images is obtained through the Section Analysis in the NanoScope Analysis software (Bruker, Germany). Then the height of NH2-GQDs is calculated by measuring the difference value from baseline to the peak in the height profile. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired by using a Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA) from 400 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on an ESCALAB 250 surface analysis system (Thermo, USA) with an Al Kα 280 eV excitation source. UV–vis spectra were measured on a U-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) using a 1.0 cm optical path. FL spectra were measured on an F-7000 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) with a 0.5 cm quartz cuvette.

A series of standard Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer solution (pH 3.0–12.0, 0.04 M) were prepared using the mixed acid solution (0.04 M) containing acetic acid, boric acid, and phosphoric acid with the addition of various amounts of NaOH (0.2 M), which were adjusted with a pH meter [27]. The pH detection was achieved using NH2-GQDs as a probe. A series of pH solution (pH 3.0–12.0, 200 μL) was added into NH2-GQDs (1 mg mL−1, 100 μL), following the addition of 100 μL of deionized water. Then, the mixed solution reacted for 30 min at 37 °C with a slight shake subsequently. Finally, the FL spectra of λem430 were recorded with a 1.0 cm optical path, under an excitation wavelength of 280 nm with a PMT voltage of 500 V and slit of 10 nm. The linear calibrations were obtained by plotting the pH value versus the FL intensity at 430 nm.

A fresh stock solution of TA (10 mM) was prepared, and then diluted to working concentration. The FL turn-off detection of TA was realized by following steps. First, a series of TA solution (final concentration: 0–50 μM, 200 μL) was added into NH2-GQDs (1 mg mL−1, 100 μL), following the addition of 100 μL of 0.04 M BR buffer (pH 9). Then, the mixed solution reacted for 30 min at 37 °C with a slight shake subsequently. Finally, the FL spectra of λem430 were recorded with a 1.0 cm optical path, under an excitation wavelength of 280 nm with a PMT voltage of 500 V and slit of 10 nm. The linear calibrations were obtained by plotting the concentration of TA (1–40 μM) and logarithm of FL intensity at 430 nm (log F). The selectivity for TA was investigated by adding other biomolecules and metal ions at identical conditions.

The amino-functionalized GQDs (NH2-GQDs) are synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal approach using GO as carbon source and NH3·H2O as nitrogen modifier, while the GQDs are synthesized without the using of NH3·H2O. During the synthesis process, potassium hydroxide (KOH) serves as a cutting agent, and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is employed as an assistant scissor, which synergistically cause multiple defects to GQDs. Besides, alkali cutting renders GQDs with enrichment of both free and bound edges [28]. Thus, the covalent functionalization can be achieved with these enriched edges, where amino groups are modified on GQDs via amidation between NH3·H2O and carboxyl groups (-COOH) (Scheme 1). TEM images of GQDs demonstrate monodisperse nanodots with the average diameter of 3.0 nm in lateral size (Fig. 1 A). After functionalized with amino groups, the average size of NH2-GQDs (6.4 nm) is larger than that of the pristine GQDs (Fig. 1B). The AFM images of GQDs and NH2-GQDs present uniform particles with the identical size as illustrated in TEM results (Fig. 1A and B), while the average height of NH2-GQDs increases from 1.288 nm (Figure 1C) to 1.828 nm (Fig. 1D) compared with GQDs. All these results confirm the successful covalent functionalization of amino groups on the surface of GQDs by means of amidation. FT-IR is used to demonstrate the variation of the functional groups on the surface of GQDs and NH2-GQDs (Fig. 1E). The wide band (3422 cm−1) in GQDs is attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H, and the band at 1593 cm−1 is ascribed to the skeletal vibration of CC in aromatic structure [29]. Moreover, the symmetrical and antisymmetrical stretching vibrations of COO− in GQDs are situated in 1329 cm−1 and 1427 cm−1, respectively. In addition to these bands, a weak broad band of N–H stretching vibration appears at 3520 cm−1 and a stretching vibration of C–N locates at 1335 cm−1 in NH2-GQDs. The absorption peak at 1695 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of amide bonds (CO–NH) [30]. Besides, only one stretching vibration of COO− (1422 cm−1) with weaker intensity exists in NH2-GQDs, indicating the consumption of COOH groups after the formation of amide bonds. We further confirm the formation of GQDs and NH2-GQDs by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1F, the XPS patterns of GQDs split into four typical peaks, including C–C (284.4 eV), CC (284.8 eV), C–O–C/C–OH (286.1 eV) and COOH (288.9 eV) [31]. Besides the deconvoluted peaks containing in GQDs, an obvious peak associated with C–N at 285.2 eV appears in NH2-GQDs (Fig. 1G). In order to confirm the configuration of N in NH2-GQDs, high resolution N1s spectra of NH2-GQDs is conducted (Fig. 1H). Only an obvious peak corresponding to the amino (399.3 eV) N atoms is recognized, which reveals the presence and definite configuration of N in NH2-GQDs [29,32].

The optical properties of NH2-GQDs were thoroughly investigated. As shown in Figure S1, NH2-GQDs display an obvious UV–vis absorption at 225 nm, which is assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. Another weak shoulder appears at around 260 nm, which can be ascribed to the n-π* (nO 2p-π* and nN 2p-π*) transition between energy levels from the surface states produced by the amino and carboxyl functional groups [33]. Moreover, both GQDs and NH2-GQDs show a broad red-shifted shoulder peak with absorption tails in the entire visible region, possibly attributing to the self-assembled J-type aggregation [34]. To investigate the FL performance, 3D fluorescence spectroscopy analysis was carried out to present 2D fluorescence topographical maps, showing the concentration-dependent shift of fluorescent emission centers and intensity of NH2-GQDs (Figure S2). When the concentration of NH2-GQDs is lower than 0.5 mg mL−1 (0.1 and 0.25 mg mL−1), a maximum obvious emission center (C1) is displayed with the optimal excitation/emission wavelength of 270 nm/450 nm, accompanied by some relatively weak irregular emission areas including C2, C3 and C4 within the excitation/emission wavelength at around ∼330 nm/∼450 nm, ∼370 nm/450 nm and ∼430 nm/∼500 nm, respectively (Figure S2A and B). Besides, the FL intensity of each emission area enhances with the increase of concentration. As the concentration reaches to 0.5 mg mL−1 (Figure S2C), the emission center in C2 and C3 become obvious and regular with enhanced FL intensity, in which the maximum emission center locates at C3 area with optimal excitation/emission wavelength of 420 nm/490 nm, and the irregular emission area of C4 also shows an increased intensity. Whereas the emission center at C1 within the excitation and emission wavelength at around ∼280 nm and ∼450 nm turn to be irregular with declined intensity. For the NH2-GQDs with concentration of 1 mg mL−1 (Figure S2D), the maximum regular emission center shifts to C4 area (the optimal excitation/emission wavelength is 430 nm/500 nm) with higher FL intensity, while the other emission areas of C1, C2 and C3 becomes more irregular with declined FL intensity. When the concentrations are as high as 2 mg mL−1 and 5 mg mL−1 (Figure S2E and F), only a prominent emission center of C4 with the optimal excitation/emission wavelength of 430 nm/500 nm is observed. Moreover, several emission areas with shorter excitation/emission wavelength disappears gradually. For instance, one emission center (C1) disappear in the concentrations of 2 mg mL−1, while both the two centers (C1 and C2) vanish at concentrations as high as 5 mg mL−1 condition. This increased concentration-induced redshift emission center with improved intensity might be attributed to the self-assembled J-type aggregation effect. The aggregation process of NH2-GQDs is monitored by AFM images (Fig. 2 ). In a dilute solution (0.25 mg mL−1), NH2-GQDs are sparse and monodispersed with an average topographic height of around 1.482 nm (Fig. 2A). As the concentration increases (1.0 mg mL−1), a slight aggregation tendency is observed with enhanced average height (4.05 nm), indicating that multiple NH2-GQDs might be stacked layer by layer (Fig. 2B). With the further increase of the concentration (5 mg mL−1), larger aggregates are formed with sharp increase of average height (8.91 nm) (Fig. 2C). The result demonstrates that the morphologies of NH2-GQDs tend to assembly with a J-type small aggregates as the concentration increases, decreasing the solvation of the hydrophilic functional groups, which can reduces the non-radiative decay of NH2-GQDs and cause the enhanced FL intensity. In addition, as the increase of the concentration, more and more NH2-GQDs will be stacked together to enlarge the graphene basal plane that possess smaller bandgaps than those in a single NH2-GQDs, thus leading to the maximum of the emission wavelength with a redshift (Fig. 2D).

Furthermore, the NH2-GQDs show the obvious excitation-dependent FL behaviors. When the concentration of NH2-GQDs is lower than 0.5 mg mL−1, the FL intensity gradually decreases along with a red shift emission as the excitation wavelength increases. As the concentration is higher than 0.5 mg mL−1, the FL intensity initially increases and then decreases remarkably accompanied by a red-shift of the peaks with the progressively increasing excitation wavelength (Figure S3). The excitation-dependent FL behavior of NH2-GQDs with multi-fluorescent emission centers might be attributed to the different types of electronic transitions dominated by the graphene basal plane (sp2 CC) and abundant functional groups (such as –COOH, -COO- and –NH2). To confirm the different types of electronic transitions, FL excitation spectra have been measured and deconvoluted using a Gaussian fit (Figure S4), presenting four consecutive peaks (∼280 nm, ∼330 nm, 370 nm and ∼430 nm). It has been proved that the high energy peak at ∼280 nm is attributed to the amino-related nN2p–σ* transition, while the peak at ∼330 nm is ascribed to the π–π* transition in the aromatic sp2 domain. In addition, the peaks at around 370 nm and 430 nm are assigned to the electronic transitions of carboxyl (–COOH) and carboxylate (–COO−) groups, respectively. Moreover, with the increase of the concentration (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 and 5.0 mg mL−1), there is a slight redshift in the peaks of the transitions, in which the peaks at shorter wavelengths (∼280 nm and ∼330 nm) are weakened and even disappear gradually, accompanied by an enhanced peaks of transition at ∼370 nm and ∼430 nm. The above results indicate that under lower concentration (lower than 0.5 mg mL−1), the activity of amino groups can play a leading role in controlling the excitation and emission, while the dominant control of excitation and emission is mainly carboxyl groups and the effect of amino groups can be inhibited in higher NH2-GQDs concentration. It is worth noting that the percentages of the four transitions are roughly the same when the NH2-GQDs concentration is 0.5 mg mL−1, suggesting that all the functional groups are contributed equally to the excitation and emission. In our work, the following detection of pH and TA both relies on the functional amino groups, thus, the low concentration of NH2-GQDs seems to be more suitable for the further sensing system.

Compared with GQDs (2.5%), NH2-GQDs have higher FL quantum yield (7.4%) (detailed description is depicted in Supporting Information, Table S1), due to the eliminating of the redundant oxygen-containing groups via surface functionalization of amino groups with strong electron withdrawing ability on GQDs. In addition, NH2-GQDs exhibit long-term stability against ionic strength and illumination (Figure S5). The FL intensity of NH2-GQDs cannot be disturbed even the KCl concentration high up to 2.0 M (Figure S5A). Moreover, the FL intensity of NH2-GQDs will not be influenced under continuous xenon lamp irradiation for 7200 s, whereas the intensity of fluorescein declines rapidly under the same conditions (Figure S5B). These results demonstrate the favorable and tunable optical behaviors of NH2-GQDs and their potential application in the fields of biosensing and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of NH2-GQDs toward pH
ce:section.ce:para: The FL response of GQDs and NH2-GQDs (1 mg mL−1) under various pH condition were explored. As shown in Fig. 3 A, The FL intensity of NH2-GQDs enhances rapidly from pH 3.0 to pH 10.0, and declines after pH 10.0, indicating a pH-dependent FL behavior of NH2-GQDs. Compared with NH2-GQDs, GQDs exhibit no apparent pH variation within pH 3.0–12.0 (Fig. 3C and D). The pH-dependent FL behavior reflects the FL mechanism of NH2-GQDs. On one hand, the FL intensity of NH2-GQDs originates from free zigzag sites with a carbene-like triplet ground state of the graphene basal plane [35]. More free edge-enriched zigzag sites are produced in alkaline condition, which contributes to an enhanced FL intensity [28]. However, the zigzag sites will be protonated and interact with H+ to form a reversible complex, which break the emission and low down the intensity. On the other hand, the protonation state of NH2-GQDs can also affect the pH response. Based on the plenty amino and carboxylic groups on the surface of GQDs, the deprotonation degree of NH2-GQDs increases gradually as pH change from acid to alkali, along with the generation of high level of deprotonated nitrogen and oxygen atoms on the surface of NH2-GQDs, which thus changes the electron distributions. In this work, owning to the synergy effect including protonation of functional groups and intrinsic free zigzag sites [5], NH2-GQDs exhibit maximum FL intensity at pH 10.0. This result suggests that NH2-GQDs exhibit superior FL performance in alkali condition, and have potential as fluorescent sensors for pH measurement. A linear relationship between the FL intensity of NH2-GQDs and pH value is developed with regression equation of F = 953.5 pH - 108.58 (pH 3.0–10.0, R2 = 0.9856) (Fig. 3B).

In order to assess the potential feasibility of the pH sensing probe, the determination of pH is achieved by testing the real samples (tap water, beer, lake water and snow water). For the measurement, NH2-GQDs were added into the solution of real samples and the FL intensity was recorded under the excitation of 280 nm. The pH was calculated based on the FL response of NH2-GQDs under different pH condition, which was presented in Table S2. The results are well consistent with those acquired by measuring on a pH meter, further demonstrating the reliability of the NH2-GQDs probe for pH sensing.

For the detection of TA by using NH2-GQDs as a probe, the FL spectra of NH2-GQDs under different concentration of TA were carried out, showing that the FL intensity of NH2-GQDs rapidly decreases upon the attaching of TA (Fig. 4 A). To investigate the detection mechanism, the interaction between NH2-GQDs and TA has been discussed as follow. TA is rich in phenolic hydroxyl with aromatic structure, which is prone to complex with NH2-GQDs through hydrogen bonding with the surface oxygen functional groups and π-π stacking interaction between aromatic groups of TA and NH2-GQDs [24,36–38]. Moreover, the amino groups on the surface of NH2-GQDs promote the electrostatic interaction between TA and NH2-GQDs. The formation of NH2-GQDs-TA complex might inhibit the activity of zigzag sites as well as the amino groups at the edge of GQDs, thus resulting in the FL quenching of NH2-GQDs. Then, the experimental parameters affecting the detection of TA, e.g., concentration of NH2-GQDs, pH value, incubating temperature and time, were thoroughly investigated as displayed in Figure S6. Therefore, at the optimal detecting condition, a nanosensor for FL turn-off determination of TA content is developed by illuminating the linear relationship between TA concentration and the logarithm of the FL intensity (log F) of NH2-GQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 4B, linear calibration ranges are obtained within 1–40 μM and a limit of detection of 43.3 nM (3σ/s, n = 9) is achieved, with a precision of 0.08% RSD at a concentration level of 5 μM (n = 9). In comparison with previous reported TA detection methods, our work possesses relatively higher sensitivity and wider linear range (Table S3). Furthermore, the selectivity of this NH2-GQDs probe toward TA (50 μM) was evaluate under 100 μM other foreign species including metal ions, anions and biomolecules (Figure S7). The value of log F of NH2-GQDs in the presence of TA (50 μM) declines, while no obvious value of log F variation is observed under 100 μM of other foreign species under pH 9.0 condition. The result indicates that NH2-GQDs exhibit excellent selectivity for TA determination against other interferences.

Moreover, the practicability of this NH2-GQDs probe for the quantification of TA in a series of beer samples has also been carried out. Briefly, 10-fold diluted beer solution (Tsingtao Beer, purchased from local supermarket) was mixed with NH2-GQDs solution under optimal conditions. Then the FL intensity was recorded under excitation of 280 nm. The content of TA was achieved from the calibration of TA and log F, and the results were summarized in Table S4. Subsequently the results are verified by spiking recovery experiments, which is realized via the addition of a certain level of standard TA solution to the checking samples with same procedures. The recoveries are achieved from 95.26% to 99.27%. The satisfactory results further validate the practicality and reliability of this NH2-GQDs probe for simultaneous detection of pH and TA.

In summary, we have developed a simple one-pot alkali cutting method to obtain FL tunable amino-functionalized GQDs (NH2-GQDs), exhibiting brighter FL emission than GQDs and possessing concentration- and excitation-dependent behaviors as well. Specifically, NH2-GQDs show a unique pH-sensitive FL property rather than GQDs, which can be employed as a probe for pH determination. Moreover, a complex is formed between NH2-GQDs and TA via π-π stacking interaction and electrostatic interaction, leading to the FL quenching of NH2-GQDs. Based on this, a FL turn-off quantitative determination method of TA is established with high sensitivity and selectivity. Remarkably, the proposed pH and TA sensing probe shows considerable practicality and reliability in real sample assay. Overall, the proposed NH2-GQDs hold a great potential as a multifunctional nanoprobe for multiple targeted analytes determination, providing a promising candidate for biosensing in the future studies.

Xueying Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Original draft preparation. Jiayu Yu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Original draft preparation. Yongcun Yan: Investigation. Weiling Song: Investigation. Xin Hai: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully appreciate the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22004077), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671988), the Open Project of Chemistry Department of Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX201928), and the Open Project of Shandong Key Laboratory of Tumor Marker Detection Technology (KLDTTM2019-4).
